<!-- Topic 1: Course Overview -->
<!-- Total slides: 19 -->

# Course Overview

---

## Structured programming — and why it still matters {.smaller}

+ This class is not about memorizing syntax — it is about learning to think precisely.
+ By the end of this course, you will write C++ programs that work, and more importantly, you will understand *why* they work.

::: notes
Total slides: 19
First-day framing slide. Set the intellectual tone before covering any content. Syntax is the surface — reasoning is the substance. Pause here and let the question land before moving forward.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The goal of this class {.smaller}

+ Start your journey to becoming a professional programmer.
+ Teach you how to think logically and precisely.
+ Learn how to master a domain of knowledge.

::: notes
Three goals that are bigger than "learn C++." The third one — mastering a domain — is worth dwelling on: learning to code teaches students how to go deep in any subject, not just programming. That skill transfers everywhere.
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## What this class is about {.smaller}

+ Structured programming using C++.
+ Learning to reason logically — not just getting code to run.
+ Building the foundations that professional programmers rely on.

::: notes
Signal early: students who focus only on making code compile will struggle. Students who focus on understanding will improve quickly. Syntax is just the surface representation of thought.
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Programming is fun {.smaller}

+ You build things that did not exist before you started.
+ A working program you wrote yourself is satisfying in a way that is hard to describe.
+ The frustration of debugging and the relief of fixing it are both part of the experience.

::: notes
Set the tone: this should be enjoyable. Not every minute — debugging is genuinely hard — but the overall experience of getting better at something difficult is rewarding. Students who find it fun will practice more; students who practice more will improve faster.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Programming is intellectually challenging {.smaller}

+ Programming requires holding multiple ideas in your head simultaneously.
+ Every bug is a puzzle. Every working program is a small proof that your reasoning was correct.
+ The difficulty is the point — it is what makes the skill valuable.

::: notes
Counter the expectation that programming should feel easy. Struggle is evidence of growth. A student who is confused is a student who is encountering something real.
:::

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Programming is economically valuable {.smaller}

+ Software developers consistently rank among the highest-paid technical professionals.
+ Demand for programmers is not declining — it is expanding into every industry.
+ Learning to program opens doors that are closed to people who cannot.

::: notes
Keep this grounded and honest. The point is not "you will be rich" — the point is that this skill has real-world leverage. The present is a good time to be learning this.
[Graphic suggestion: BLS Occupational Outlook data for software developers. Strong growth projections through 2030s.]
:::

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Programming skills transfer {.smaller}

+ Every profession that requires thinking benefits from the habits programming builds.
+ Decomposing a problem, reasoning about sequences, and testing assumptions are not programming skills — they are thinking skills.
+ Good employees go to training. Learning a second language is training.

::: notes
The "good for any job you need to think in" point from the original lecture. Expand on this: coding is to this generation what law school was to a previous one — a rigorous training in logical thinking.
:::

<!-- Slide 7 -->

---

## Teaching thinking has changed {.smaller}

+ Your grandparents were told: go to law school to learn how to think.
+ Your generation is learning to code to learn how to think.
+ Still true in the world of GenAI — AI amplifies thinking; it does not replace it.

::: notes
One of the most important slides in this section. The point is not that C++ is useful — the point is that structured thinking is a fundamental skill, and coding is how this generation acquires it. The GenAI point is worth dwelling on: a student who understands how to think precisely will know what to ask AI and how to verify what it gives back. That is a durable skill.
:::

<!-- Slide 8 -->

---

## Programming trains a way of thinking {.smaller}

+ **Abstraction** — simplifying complex problems into manageable pieces.
+ **Decomposition** — breaking a large problem into smaller, solvable parts.
+ **Algorithmic reasoning** — describing a sequence of steps that reliably produces a result.

::: notes
These three habits are the intellectual payoff of this course. Students may not feel them developing in the first few weeks, but they are the reason the course exists. These skills apply to engineering, medicine, law, business — any field where problems need to be solved systematically.
:::

<!-- Slide 9 -->

---

## What is C++? {.smaller}

+ A modern, high-performance language used in systems, games, embedded software, and performance-critical applications.
+ Designed to give the programmer control — over memory, over performance, over abstraction level.
+ NOT the same as C. C++ is a distinct language with its own rules and features.

::: notes
Do not get technical. Focus on *why C++ still exists* — because there are domains where performance and control matter more than convenience. Briefly acknowledge that students may have heard of Python or Java; C++ is harder but teaches more about how computers actually work.
[Graphic suggestion: C++ ISO logo image — images/p360_ISO_C++_Logo.png]
:::

<!-- Slide 10 -->

---

## C++ has layers {.smaller}

+ C++ can look like Python or Java at the high abstraction level — clean, readable, safe.
+ It can also work close to the hardware — managing memory directly, accessing hardware.
+ Most languages give you one level. C++ gives you all of them.

::: notes
Reassure students: we will not be thrown into the deep end. This course works in the Mechanisms layer — the C++ features that interact with hardware. The diagram on the next slide shows where that sits.
:::

<!-- Slide 11 -->

---

## Where does p360 fit? {.smaller}

| Layer | What it covers | Course |
|-------|----------------|--------|
| SW Engineering | Design choices, architecture | p400 |
| Abstractions | Why we avoid direct hardware access | p400 |
| **Mechanisms** | **C++/C: direct hardware access** | **p360** |
| ASM | Machine-level programming | — |

::: notes
This four-layer stack diagram is from the original lecture. p360 lives in the Mechanisms layer — we learn how C++ accesses and manages hardware directly. p400 builds above that into object-oriented design and abstraction. Knowing where you are in the stack gives students a frame for why the material is what it is.
[Graphic suggestion: redraw the hand-drawn stack diagram from the original lecture as a clean vertical stack with left-side brackets labeling p360 and p400 scope.]
:::

<!-- Slide 12 -->

---

## Does it matter I'm not learning X language? {.smaller}

+ It is difficult to determine which language is the most popular — indices like TIOBE measure different things and disagree.
+ What matters is the language your employer uses.
+ Even that doesn't fully matter — good employees go to training, and a programmer who knows one language well learns the next one quickly.

::: notes
Students often ask why C++ and not Python. The honest answer: the specific language matters less than learning to think like a programmer. A student who understands structured programming in C++ will pick up Python in weeks. The reverse is harder.
Reference: TIOBE Index — https://www.tiobe.com/tiobe-index/
:::

<!-- Slide 13 -->

---

## Structured Programming {.smaller}

+ A programming style focused on **data** and **transforming** that data.
+ Programs are built from three control structures: **sequence**, **selection**, and **iteration**.
+ Everything you write in this course fits inside one of those three ideas.

::: notes
This is the intellectual spine of the semester. Students will encounter sequence in week one, selection (if/else) within a few weeks, and iteration (loops) shortly after. Every program they will ever write — no matter how complex — is a combination of these three structures. Say that out loud.
:::

<!-- Slide 14 -->

---

## Object-Oriented Programming {.smaller}

+ A programming style focused on **relationships** between interacting entities.
+ Programs are organized around objects — things that know their own data and behavior.
+ We will cover OOP in the second half of this course; structured programming comes first.

::: notes
Signal explicitly: don't worry about OOP yet. It is a second lens on programming, not a replacement. Structured programming is the foundation; OOP builds on it. The course is designed so that SP comes first and OOP is not floating without a base.
:::

<!-- Slide 15 -->

---

## What you will be able to do {.smaller}

+ Read and write correct C++ programs from scratch.
+ Trace program execution step by step — not guess, but *know* what a program does.
+ Reason about variables, memory, and control flow.
+ Use professional tools and workflows.

::: notes
Concrete outcomes, not abstract goals. Students should leave this slide with a mental image of what success looks like at the end of the semester. Tracing is worth emphasizing: a student who can trace is a student who can debug.
:::

<!-- Slide 16 -->

---

## Write code — lots of it {.smaller}

+ Programming is a physical skill, not just an intellectual one.
+ You cannot learn to swim by reading about swimming.
+ The most important thing you will do in this class is write code.

::: notes
The message is simple: the path to competence is practice. The lecture content is the map; the coding is the territory. Students who only watch lectures will not improve. Students who code every day will. Do not soften this.
:::

<!-- Slide 17 -->

---

## How this class works {.smaller}

+ My role: explain, guide, challenge.
+ Your role: practice, think, ask questions.
+ Learning is iterative — mistakes are expected, not failures.

::: notes
Set the partnership tone. This is not passive content delivery. Students who wait to understand before they try will wait a long time. Students who try, fail, and ask questions will learn fast. Make it safe to be confused out loud.
:::

<!-- Slide 18 -->

---

## Summary {.smaller}

+ This course uses C++ to teach structured thinking — the goal is reasoning, not syntax.
+ Programming is fun, intellectually demanding, economically valuable, and transferable to any thinking profession.
+ The three control structures — sequence, selection, and iteration — are all you need to build anything.

<!-- Slide 19 -->
